panels.description: Total requests processed by the resilient API
panels.title: Total requests (resilient)

panels.description: HTTP-level success rate — only counts requests that actually returned 4xx/5xx. Mid-retry increments are excluded so Resilient is not penalised for recovering.
panels.title: Success rate (resilient) — HTTP only

panels.description: Circuit breaker state: 1 = open (bad), 0 = closed (healthy)
panels.title: Circuit breaker state

panels.description: Mean time to recovery for incidents
panels.title: Incident MTTR

panels.description: CPU % over time — spikes here correlate directly with /chaos/cpu injections from the ramp test.
panels.title: CPU pressure — node + per-API share (chaos ramp)

panels.description: Memory % over time — spikes correlate with /chaos/memory injections. Watch Baseline degrade faster than Resilient.
panels.title: Memory pressure — node + per-API share (chaos ramp)

panels.description: Error rate per stage over time. Watch Fragile spike first, then Baseline under heavy chaos. Resilient should stay near 0% because it only increments errors on final HTTP failure.
panels.title: Error rate under chaos ramp — Fragile / Resilient / Baseline

panels.description: p95 latency per stage during chaos ramp. Resilient's circuit breaker should keep p95 below 2s even when CPU is saturated. Fragile and Baseline will climb.
panels.title: p95 latency under chaos ramp — Fragile / Resilient / Baseline

panels.description: Fragile API — uses rate() over 5m window so the stat reflects current behaviour, not cumulative totals which mask recovery.
panels.title: Fragile API — success rate (current)

panels.description: Resilient API — uses rate() so mid-retry api_errors_total increments do not inflate the error count. Only final HTTP failures count.
panels.title: Resilient API — success rate (current)
Error rate — Fragile (red) vs Resilient (green) vs Baseline (orange)
p95 latency — Fragile (red) vs Resilient (green) vs Baseline (orange)
Throughput — Fragile (red) vs Resilient (green) vs Baseline (orange)

panels.description: Three-way success rate using rate() — compares current window, not all-time totals.
panels.title: Baseline API — success rate (no AI)

panels.description: SLO adherence ratio from the rolling 100-request window. This is the honest measure — it includes latency SLO breaches that the HTTP success rate misses.
panels.title: SLO adherence — Resilient / Baseline / Fragile

panels.description: SLO adherence over time — shows how each stage degrades under chaos pressure and recovers when it passes.
panels.title: SLO adherence over time
Chaos exposure ratio — Baseline (orange) vs Resilient (green)
panels.description: Ratio of bad stressors drawn per API. Resilient's AI weighting should keep this lower than Baseline's uniform random draw.

panels.title: Retry exhaustion — Baseline vs Resilient
panels.description: Baseline exhausted = all retries failed. Resilient degraded = fell back to degraded response. Resilient should degrade more gracefully (lower rate).
Success rate per API stage — uses rate() so current performance is shown, not all-time averages which hide chaos events.
panels.title: Success rate by stage (%) — current 5m window

panels.description: Request rate per stage over time
panels.title: Request rate by stage

panels.description: Current success rate per function type. realtime_query should be lowest (strict 100ms SLO). llm_inference should be highest (tolerant 3000ms SLO).
panels.title: Adaptive — success rate by function type

panels.description: SLO breaches over time. realtime_query will breach most (100ms SLO). llm_inference will breach least (3000ms SLO). This is the key differentiation panel.
panels.title: Adaptive — SLO breaches by function type

panels.description: p95 latency per function type. Should clearly separate: realtime ~80ms, data_processing ~500ms, llm_inference ~2000ms. Three distinct bands = three distinct workload characters.
panels.title: Adaptive — p95 latency by function type

panels.description: Workload character summary — median latency vs SLO limit for each function type. The gap between bar and SLO threshold line tells you how close to the edge each workload runs.
panels.title: Workload character — median latency vs SLO threshold

panels.description: SLO breach rate per function type as a percentage of requests. realtime_query should be highest (strictest SLO), llm_inference lowest (most tolerant). A flat chart here means your SLOs need tightening.
panels.title: SLO breach rate (absolute) by function type — differentiation proof

panels.description: Chaos profile weights — watch these shift when consecutive failures trigger adaptation. The drift-back every 10s creates visible oscillation.
panels.title: Adaptive — chaos profile weights per function type

panels.description: Which recovery strategy fired for each function type. data_processing should use retry most. llm_inference should use fallback_model and cache. realtime_query should use degrade (no retries — too slow).
panels.title: Adaptive — recovery strategy usage by function type

panels.description: Stressor distribution per function type — confirms each function is being hit with a distinct stressor mix.
panels.title: Adaptive — stressor distribution per function type

panels.description: Rolling success rate (last 100 requests) per function type. Watch the oscillation caused by profile adaptation then drift-back. Expect realtime_query to be most volatile.
panels.title: Adaptive — rolling success rate per function type
